ontology.foodex2_description: Fruit and vegetable juices and smoothies
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fruit and vegetable juices and smoothies as a category; no narrower smoothie-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for generic fruit smoothie was found in the published SNOMED CT food hierarchy as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of smoothie is one medium cup of approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing around 160 kcal, 36.5 g carbohydrate, and 24.5 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Smoothie, fruit — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fruit smoothie (generic), which reports energy 64 kcal, protein 1.1 g, fat 0.2 g, carbohydrate 14.6 g, sugar 9.8 g, sodium 34 mg, dietary fibre 0.2 g, calcium 40 mg, iron 0.1 mg per 100 g. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 0.0 g based on the negligible total fat content (0.2 g/100 g) and the fruit-only composition. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for a generic blended fruit smoothie was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 59 (medium) based on the closest analogous entry: mixed fruit juice / blended fruit beverage with similar sugar composition (9.8 g/100 g) and negligible fibre (0.2 g/100 g). This is consistent with published GI values for fruit-based beverages in the range 55–65 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (59 × 36.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 21.5 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.8 g/100 g) in liquid form with negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g), exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold associated with insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure risk (AHA 2018); sodium is low at 34 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fruit smoothie intake to small portions (≤150 ml) and prefer whole-fruit smoothies with fibre >2 g/100 g and no added sugar to reduce the sugar-driven blood pressure risk pathway.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.8 g/100 g combined with negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g) and liquid form results in rapid glucose absorption and significant postprandial glycaemic excursion; medium GI of approximately 59 and high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~21.5) make this beverage problematic for blood glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit generic fruit smoothies; if consumed, portion should not exceed 100 ml, and the recipe should be modified to include protein (e.g., Greek yogurt) and fibre (e.g., whole fruit, chia seeds) to attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat content is negligible (0.2 g/100 g, saturated fat ~0 g), which is favourable; however, high free sugar content (9.8 g/100 g) in liquid form is associated with elevated triglycerides, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly (WHO 2015 sugar guidelines).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, fruit smoothies should be consumed occasionally and in small portions (≤150 ml); whole-fruit versions with no added sugar and fibre >2 g/100 g are preferred to minimise sugar-driven dyslipidaemia risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mixed fruits provide vitamin C and antioxidants with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is negligible; however, fibre is very low at 0.2 g/100 g and added sugar may promote inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For rheumatoid arthritis, choose whole-fruit smoothies using anti-inflammatory fruits (berries, citrus) with ginger or turmeric, and omit added honey or sugar to avoid refined sugar-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A smoothie is a thick, blended beverage made from fresh fruits and/or vegetables, typically prepared by blending whole or chopped produce without cooking. The texture ranges from pourable to thick and creamy depending on the fruit composition and the presence of added ingredients such as yogurt, milk, or ice. The flavour profile is naturally sweet and fruity, with the sweetness driven primarily by the natural sugars of the fruit base. Per 100 g, a generic fruit smoothie provides approximately 64 kcal, 1.1 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 14.6 g carbohydrate, 9.8 g sugar, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and 34 mg sodium. Calcium is present at 40 mg/100 g and iron at 0.1 mg/100 g. The high sugar content (9.8 g/100 g) combined with very low dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g) means that glucose from the smoothie is absorbed rapidly, producing a medium glycaemic index of approximately 59 and a high glycaemic load per standard 250 ml serving (GL ~21.5). This makes generic fruit smoothies a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension, where liquid sugar intake should be minimised. The beverage is naturally low in fat and sodium, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderation and without added sugar. Smoothies are widely consumed as snacks, breakfast accompaniments, or post-exercise recovery drinks. They are vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free in their basic fruit-only form. Nutritional quality improves significantly when whole fruits are used (retaining fibre), protein sources are added (Greek yogurt, nut butter), and no honey or sugar is added.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a smoothie?
Is smoothie good for weight loss?
Can I drink smoothie if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a fruit smoothie?
How much carbohydrate is in a smoothie?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per input record), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All eight reported nutrients are preserved from the HPB source. Saturated fat is estimated at 0.0 g based on negligible total fat (0.2 g/100 g) and fruit-only composition, which is defensible. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed framework, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (saturated_fat_g) is reported as 0.0 g: this is a confirmed trace / negligible value consistent with the total fat of 0.2 g/100 g in a fruit-only blended beverage; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported by the HPB Foodo database for this generic smoothie entry and are not available from a secondary approved source for this exact food as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 59 is an analogous estimate based on mixed fruit beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for a generic blended fruit smoothie was found. 4. This is a generic record representing a typical fruit smoothie; actual nutritional values will vary depending on fruit composition, added ingredients, and preparation method. 5. The food is not cuisine-specific (International / Generic); therefore no mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules of PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. 6. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 (fruit smoothie) is applied as an exact match. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fruit and vegetable juices and smoothies) is applied as closest_parent match; no narrower smoothie-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy. 7. SNOMED CT code is null as no specific concept for generic fruit smoothie was found in the published SNOMED CT food hierarchy as of 2024.
